data.title: Advanced Micro Devices: AI Future Needs More Answers (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I’ve recently written a piece about Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) before the earnings, and while I don’t like to update a thesis on such short notice, I also think that, as my perspective on the stock has changed, I owe my subscribers the explanations and reasoning behind that change.

The main thesis for the buy rating was based on three pillars:

  • Client and Embedded segments were showing signs of improvement and could be accretive in 2025.

  • Gaming segment weakness was already priced in, and with revenue share at low levels, it wouldn’t be particularly important.

  • AI accelerator revenue consensus for 2025 was already at levels that I considered low enough to be met, and the earnings should bring some clarity on that.

Fast-forward to today, I guess I was right about the first two and not so much about the last one. Unfortunately, AI revenue estimates were probably the most important topic for the buy thesis.

Non-AI Business

To begin with, let’s take a look at the good news from the Q4 earnings call.

Client segment revenue was $2.13 billion, up 23% quarter over quarter and 58% year over year. This segment has the most operational leverage for AMD, implying that margins are sensitive to revenue levels. In the case of growing revenue, operating income grows faster, as we’ve seen, with a 62% increase quarterly and 711% year over year.

There is, of course, seasonality in this, as the end of the year is historically good for client sales. For Q1, Lisa guided the client segment to decline more than the corporate average of 7%. In that case, operating income should fall by a larger percentage as well.

I was probably too conservative in my Client revenue projections, estimating $7.5 billion, as AMD is guiding strong growth from today’s $7 billion levels.

For the year, management is confident that 2025 will be another year of share gains:

Looking into 2025, we are planning for the PC TAM to grow by a mid-single-digit percentage year-on-year. Based on the breadth of our leadership client CPU portfolio and strong design win momentum, we believe we can grow client segment revenue well ahead of the market.

In the gaming segment, revenue was up about 22% quarter over quarter, coming from very low levels. Operating profit, however, accounted for only 2.5% of the total, too low to be meaningful.

Year over year, both semicustom and GPU gaming revenue were down. For semicustom, Sony (SONY) and Microsoft (MSFT) focused on lowering inventory, and even with normalization, the current console generation cycle is likely nearing its end. The next generation of GPUs, based on the RDNA 4 architecture, will begin selling in March and could bring some momentum to the GPU side of the gaming business. The company expects a “modest increase” in gaming for 2025.

Embedded continues to be flattish, mostly due to weak end-market demand. Industrial exposure is showing signs of weakness across semiconductor companies, so this isn’t surprising. At least this segment has more sticky margins. For the year, Lisa also expects a “modest” recovery. In my previous analysis, I was considering $4 billion in revenue for 2025, which now seems likely given today’s level of $3.5 billion and the modest guidance.

The server side of the datacenter segment is also performing really well:

2024 marks another major inflection point for our server business, as share gains accelerated, driven by the ramp of fifth-gen EPYC Turin and strong double-digit percentage year-over-year growth in fourth-gen EPYC sales. In cloud, we exited 2024 with well over 50% share at the majority of our largest hyperscale customers.

Total datacenter revenue was $3.9 billion (up 8.7% QoQ), which includes both servers and AI GPUs. When asked whether datacenter GPU revenue could be assumed to be in the $2 billion range, CFO Jean Hu indicated that “servers did better than datacenter GPUs”, implying that server revenue was likely above $2 billion, while GPU revenue was at lower levels. Management is guiding for strong double-digit growth in the datacenter server segment.

Overall, the non-AI business had a decent quarter and client and server CPUs should continue to drive growth into 2025.

AI GPUs

Well, that’s where it becomes less interesting, not only because of the numbers itself but the lack of visibility for the year. The company met the guidance of $5 billion a year for 2024, but current AI revenue couldn't get to the two billion range per quarter just yet. Actually, datacenter revenue was around $3.9 billion, while the market was projecting $4.1 billion. And with the CPU segment outperforming, the disappointment on the GPU side was even greater.

Then, there was the guidance of datacenter revenue falling around 7% on Q1.

(...) So Q1 guide was down 7% sequentially, as Jean mentioned. And the way that breaks out in each of the segments assume that the data center would be down just about that average, so the corporate average.

Besides, the company has chosen not to disclose the AI revenue separately, which is clearly not a strength sign.

And as we go into 2025, I think our guidance will be more at the segment level with some color as to some qualitative color as to what's going on between the two businesses.

That’s also a shift from last year, as they had previously guided specifically for AI GPUs and then updated this guidance over the quarters. I believe that, given how the market was framing revenue estimates, based on AI GPUs (as I did in mine as well), this introduces a significant amount of uncertainty. For this segment, where market consensus once expected 100% growth, guiding for “strong double-digit growth” is not enough, as the range is too wide.

This added uncertainty also comes at a time when revenue is being guided downward, making it even worse.

Management also added that the MI350 product, which was originally expected in the second half of the year, is now being pulled forward to mid-year as it is driving engagement among hyperscalers, including potential new clients. While I think this is good news on its own, it is also a clear indication that the MI325 was not competitive.

The MI350 is also the reason AI GPU revenue is expected to be concentrated in the second half of the year. However, I have a hard time believing this, given the competition from NVIDIA's B200.

For me, it's hard to estimate what the final revenue will be. If we're looking at $1.7–1.8 billion today, a 7% decrease would bring it down to around $1.6 billion next quarter. With some recovery in Q2, let's say $3.4 billion for the first half of the year. So, $7 billion in AI revenue for the year still seems achievable.

Earnings Revision

If we look at the market’s projections, while the trend is showing downward revisions, estimates have not changed significantly after the last earnings call.

Doing a sensitivity analysis and considering robust growth in the client and non-AI datacenter segments, I estimate that the implied AI revenue is still above the $7 billion range, which I see as an optimistic outlook as of today. Therefore, I believe further downward revisions may occur.

Conclusion

AMD delivered solid results in absolute terms last quarter, but datacenter numbers and the lack of clarity and guidance on AI-related revenue were disappointing for me, especially since AMD has a history of issuing clear guidance and then achieving it.

Valuation seems reasonable at this point; however, I still see some degree of optimism in market consensus for revenue, as explained above. I'm downgrading the company to a Hold, especially for investors focused on the short term, as I don’t see any clear catalyst for value appreciation at the moment. On the contrary, strong results from NVIDIA (NVDA), Marvell (MRVL), and others could amplify the narrative of AMD’s lack of competitiveness and even signal clear signs of market share loss.

I would consider upgrading the company again if the implied AI GPU revenue drops below $7 billion, or if there is another correction or more information available following the next earnings report.

data.title: Estimating The Potential Impacts Of U.S. Tariffs On Economies And Markets
data.description:
U.S. trade policy remains highly fluid, with the government announcing several tariff proposals since President Donald Trump took office last month. As of this writing, the Trump administration has implemented a 10% tariff on imports from China and announced plans to enact a 25% tariff on all imported steel and aluminum products in early March. In addition, the U.S. may move forward with an earlier plan-which was put on pause last week-to tax all Canadian (excluding energy) and Mexican imports at a 25% rate beginning next month. Finally, the U.S. administration is also reportedly looking at the imposition of reciprocal tariffs (i.e., imposing the same tariffs on imports that U.S. exports face) to all U.S. trading partners.

Amid uncertainty over the potential market and economic impacts of these tariffs, our strategist team at Russell Investments has developed a model that allows us to input a wide range of different tariff scenarios-for example, a 25% U.S. tariff on Canadian imports, or blanket U.S. tariffs on all steel imports-to see what the potential impacts would be on cost structures for both U.S. and global businesses.

How could today's proposed tariffs impact the global economy?

If the current slate of proposed U.S. tariffs is implemented, our model estimates a 60-to-80 bps increase in cost structures across most sectors of the global economy. We also think it's very likely that companies will pass on this increase to customers in full, as has been the case in previous tariff episodes. To this end, we anticipate that core U.S. consumer prices-as measured by the PCE price index-would rise by about 70-80 bps.

These estimates are based on a number of inputs, as well as observations from how markets and economies reacted to the implementation of U.S. tariffs during the first Trump administration. Let's take a deeper look.

Market impacts from U.S. tariffs during the first Trump administration

During President Trump's first term in office, the U.S. implemented tariffs on steel and aluminum products from most other countries from 2018-19, as well as a wider array of tariffs on Chinese products. During this time, there was a notable decrease in correlations between different equity regions-which makes sense, given that the economic impacts of the tariffs varied by country.

Of particular note, both REITs (real estate investment trusts) and infrastructure performed well during this period, primarily due to the lower tariff risk and some discount rate movements.

Trade policy uncertainty index

A good measure of the uncertainty around U.S. tariffs during the first Trump administration can be demonstrated by looking at the so-called Trade Policy Uncertainty Index. This index crawled online news articles for words that suggested uncertainty in trade policy during this time.

As the chart shows, the index identified three main periods of elevated trade uncertainty from 2016-2020. The first was the period following Donald Trump's initial presidential election victory, in November 2016, through his first few months in the White House in early 2017. During this time, worries over potential U.S. tariffs mounted, but no policy actions were taken.

The second period, from roughly March through August 2018, is when the Trump administration finished its investigations into Chinese trade practices and announced a flurry of tariffs, with many targeting Chinese goods. The third period occurred the following year, in 2019, when the U.S. administration implemented another round of tariffs on key trading partners. Of these three periods, the second and third are the most important from an economic perspective, since that's when the tariffs were actually implemented.

So, how did asset classes perform during both times?

In the image below, the chart on the left shows how equities, real assets, bonds, foreign exchange, and government bonds performed from Feb. 28 to Aug. 1, 2018.

One of the main things that jumps out is the struggles of emerging-market equities, which is understandable given that China makes up roughly a quarter of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. On the flip side, the Russell 2000 Index of small cap stocks surged by over 10% during this time frame, with REITs also gaining by nearly as much.

The righthand side of the chart shows the performance of the same asset classes during the peak drawdown of the U.S. equity market in 2018-from March 9 to April 2-as measured by the S&P 500 Index. Once again, REITs and, to a lesser extent, infrastructure experienced far less of a decline than other assets during this timeframe.

What about in 2019? This time, there was more of a classic risk-off tone, with equity markets declining while perceived safe-havens like U.S. Treasuries and the Japanese yen did well. Yet again, REITs and infrastructure performed strongly.

Estimating the input costs of tariffs

Next, let's look at how we constructed our model for tariff input costs. We use data from the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis to see what inputs are used for each sector in the economy. The second step is to then look at the import reliance of each of these inputs. With these two steps, we then can apply tariff assumptions by country and, if necessary, product line. Product line is an important ability in cases like Canada, where the Trump administration carved out a lower tariff rate on energy resources.

The result is the below chart, which estimates the increase in input costs across U.S. equity sectors. There's an average overall increase of roughly 80 bps for U.S. sectors, with energy, materials, industrials, and consumer discretionary impacted the most. On the other side of the coin, the financials and communications sectors are estimated to be the least impacted. If we drill down further, motor vehicles, electrical equipment, and transportation in particular are the most impacted.

Chart: Estimated increase in input costs across U.S. equity sectors

The bottom line

Our research indicates that the latest proposed slate of U.S. tariffs are likely to result in a modest increase in cost structure for global organizations. As U.S. trade policy continues to evolve, we'll monitor the latest developments and share updates from our model accordingly.

Disclosures

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer, solicitation or recommendation to purchase any security.

Forecasting represents predictions of market prices and/or volume patterns utilizing varying analytical data. It is not representative of a projection of the stock market, or of any specific investment.

Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment. The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

The members of the Russell Investments group of companies are not affiliated in any manner with Frank Russell Company or any entity operating under the "FTSE RUSSELL" brand.

data.title: Apple Q1 Reinforces My Sell Thesis, But With One Massive Caveat
data.description:
In my two initial articles on Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL), I outlined some of my bearish views on the stock. I've always known this would be a contrarian view for the most part, and shareholders are more aggressive than ever about creating a floor for the stock.

Looking at the company's underwhelming Q1 report, though, I think investors are increasingly questioning the validity of the Apple bull case. Anecdotally, there are far fewer Buy calls now than in the last three years.

My take is that it won't be more than a few more quarters of underwhelming performance to tilt SA Analysts toward a Sell average. I see far more Sells and Strong Sells now than I've seen since last year.

How Did Apple Fare in Q1 2025?

Keeping in mind that Q1 is Apple's most important quarter that straddles the holiday season, the numbers weren't exactly impressive. A 4% YoY quarterly growth rate may well represent their "best quarter ever" with $124.3 billion in revenues, and their "best-ever lineup of products and services" is likely to have contributed to that, but the market was clearly disappointed with the results. AAPL saw a run-up heading into the earnings call, but that didn't last, and the stock gave up most of those gains, leaving the YTD price decline more or less intact.

Apple has rarely ever had issues with bottom-line profitability, so it's not surprising that EPS grew 10% to land at "a record $2.40". That's a great number considering it puts AAPL at a price to forward GAAP earnings multiple of just 32x. It's actually reasonably close to the median figure of 30x, making AAPL's current share price of ~$242 appear to be a bargain.

But hang on, let's keep in mind that this is a Sell thesis, not a Buy call. While these numbers may have impressed some shareholders, the reality of it might not be what you expected. To showcase that, let's look at why Q1 numbers are invariably better at the operating and net profit levels.

OpEx Control is Critical in Terms of Demonstrably Consistent Q1 Profitability

My position is that there's quite a bit of financial engineering needed to deliver strong Q1 results year after year. I think that's what investors are mistaking for long-term stability. Let me show you how this works at the operating level.

If you'll notice, Apple keeps its December quarter (the all-important Q1) operating expenses at their year's lowest, and it's no coincidence that this has been going on for several years. Both SG&A and R&D expenses are at their lowest when the iPhone has its biggest sales quarter. Every year. I find it to be counterintuitive that the strongest quarter invariably sees operating costs drop to their annual troughs.

To me, that's not a sign of healthy growth. My opinion is that Apple is doing whatever it takes to show that its profit levels are pristine when Q1 comes rolling around. It doesn't make sense for operating expenses to exhibit such an odd seasonal pattern, but a novice investor is not likely to be aware of that. All they can see is that Apple is growing its EBIT margins in a healthy way. Based on the graph above, I'd have to disagree.

There's another interesting takeaway here: Revenue slowdowns make it harder to post strong YoY operating margins in subsequent Q1s.

What do I mean by this?

Let's look at YoY quarterly revenue growth. In Q1 2023 (December 2022 quarter), the top line declined by nearly 5.5%, and yet, operating margin grew to 30.74% of revenue. The biggest reason (other than a gradual expansion at the gross margin level) is that R&D was cut to 6.6% of revenue and SG&A was cut to 5.6%. I've reproduced the same graph from above, but with line item labels for those two expenditures.

If you move forward four quarters to Q1 2024 (December 2023 quarter), you'll see that Apple needed to bring its total OpEx down to 12.11% in order to allow the operating margin to grow significantly between Q1 2023 and Q1 2024, from 30.74% to 33.76%. This happened as revenue grew just 2.1% on a YoY basis in Q1 2024. This isn't operating leverage at work; it's pure financial engineering. Perfectly above board and legitimate, but nonetheless confusing to anyone who doesn't look at the fine print.

Further ahead in the timeline, if we move forward again to Q1 2025, which is the last reported quarter, we see both SG&A and R&D down again, but to a lesser degree because top-line growth came in at 4% this time. This allowed Apple to avoid deep cuts to those two line items, but still show a 70bps increase in the operating margin. When revenue growth is strong, Apple doesn't need to micromanage its operating costs all that much because investor attention is now on the top line rather than the operating margin. They're still depressed compared to other quarters, but less so.

I'm not saying it's wrong for Apple to engage in this kind of financial engineering, but it doesn't allow shareholders to easily spot the weaknesses in the company's growth story. We already know iPhone unit sales are no longer reported, but even the weak features that Cook found it worth highlighting were definitely a disappointment:

the A18-powered devices have advanced features like extended battery life and enhanced camera capabilities, resonating with customers globally.

Seriously? Battery life and a better camera are now iPhone's most important selling points?

And now that we're aware of these operational adjustments to show growth in Q1, I have less confidence than ever about Apple being an innovator anymore.

Another upside consideration gone south: There's been a lot of talk about Apple Intelligence, but all it is, is an integration of ChatGPT and other generative models with existing features - Siri, searching photos using natural language, Messages and Mail summaries, and a few others that aren't really trailblazing features.

It shouldn't come as a surprise to any of us that Apple needs to resort to financial engineering to keep its shareholders happy. Within that context, the outlook for Q2 2025 looks impressive but is actually not. When you consider the fact that Q2 2024 showed a 4.3% revenue decline, which is the worst since Q1 2023, you suddenly realize that even if revenues were to "grow in the low- to mid-single digits year-over-year, despite a projected 2.5% foreign exchange headwind", it would only be a minor recovery of sorts and not necessarily the kind of strong growth that propels the investment narrative forward.

The Share Repurchase Angle

Apple regularly boasts its returns to shareholders via share buybacks, but the yield on those has been deteriorating over the past two years as the stock made significant gains. Moreover, the company stepped up its buyback cadence in the last two years, which makes even less sense when you look at price appreciation.

Buybacks can be a very effective tool for shareholder returns, but only when they're used judiciously and to achieve better yields over time - not worse. And yet, Apple last year announced its biggest buyback program ever, worth $110 billion - with a deal-sweetening 4% increase in the dividend.

What's clear to me is that Apple needs to use every means possible to ensure a healthy total return for its investors. In the absence of strong topline growth, the only way to do that is with aggressive buybacks and dividend yields. The only problem with that is, it hasn't been working over the past six months.

True, longer timeframes do show outperformance, but I think the glory days are now behind Apple, and going by how analysts on SA finally seem to be arriving at that consensus, I reiterate my Sell rating.

My view is that Apple is undeniably at a very mature stage in its business cycle. Without any significant innovations on the horizon (and what happened to Vision Pro, by the way? It was mentioned a grand total of 3 times on the Q1 earnings call!), the stock is going to trudge along, trying to keep up with the market by displaying its superior financial acumen Q1 after Q1. How long can that last before investors start to get anxious about their capital being stuck in a rut?

Possible Solutions to Your AAPL Problem

Long-term investors are in a particularly unique predicament because they now face long-term capital gains tax if they sell off their AAPL holding, but the reality is that they may not need to. That's what my massive caveat at the end of the article is all about.

For all others who bought Apple relatively late and whose cost basis is not too far away from the current share price, my recommendation is to Sell, then render unto Caesar what is Caesar's, and finally rotate your after-tax proceeds into a better investment.

There's also another alternative. Let's say you don't want to sell your shares outright because your cost basis is too close to or above the current share price. In that situation (or if you want to bring your cost basis further down, and you're also willing to sell at a slight premium), you can sell near-the-money covered calls.

In this example, underwriting one contract expiring at the end of February will get you a $280 premium up front on a $245 strike. If AAPL hits that level before or at contract expiry, your shares get called away at that strike price, and your total profit would be the difference between $245 and your cost basis per share, plus the premium of $2.80 per share. If your shares aren't called away, you can just roll that contract over and over, gradually bringing your cost basis down over time.

I have no doubt that AAPL has been a generational opportunity for the past several decades, but it no longer represents that. Maybe it's time to move on, but please check with your tax advisor before you make any move.

My Massive Caveat and Concluding Thoughts

I'll leave you with one major caveat here that I think long-term Apple permabulls can really get behind. If you bought the bulk of your AAPL stocks BEFORE 2010, DO NOT SELL YOUR SHARES! I want to make it amply clear that long-term investors don't need to do anything but keep holding the stock because your annual dividend yield is likely to be in the double or triple digit percentages by now.

It would be decidedly imprudent to sell an asset that's giving you that kind of yield year after year. This would then become a willable asset that your descendants can live very comfortably on.

For anyone else, I would recommend a Sell or a perpetually-rolling-over covered call options play like the one I outlined above. The sooner you can break even on your AAPL investment, the better, because that yield on cost will keep becoming juicier the more you bring down your cost basis with this strategy. If you don't want to risk your shares being called away, just go further out the money with your strike. The premiums are obviously lower, and it will take you longer to reach breakeven on your cost basis, but you get to retain your shares until they eventually start paying for themselves - over and over and year after year as long as Apple's dividends are kept intact and growing.

Although my rating is a Sell, I'm providing these alternative scenarios and strategies for different categories of Apple investors. It's a great company, no doubt, but unless you're getting a return that's superior to any of your other investments (or you create that path via options), it's not worth holding on to the stock, let alone considering a buy.

data.title: SMCY: Collecting Income From Super Micro Computer Uncertainty
data.description:
Overview

The folks over at YieldMax continue to release new funds that track different companies. One of the newer funds is YieldMax SMCI Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:SMCY), which aims to track the underlying price movements of Super Micro Computer (SMCI). SMCY is one of the newest funds and launched as recently as September 2024. Therefore, we have a smaller window of operating performance, and assets under management only total approximately $112M. Additionally, the fund's performance has been poor since its launch, which can scare investors away from considering this high-yielding fund.

SMCY currently sports an estimated annual dividend yield greater than 97%, according to the fund's overview. However, readers should note that the yield changes every month as distributions are announced. Since SMCY's inception, the price has already deteriorated by more than 42% in less than 6 months. Even with the massive dividend yield, the total return still sits around negative 20%. So why would investors even consider a fund like SMCY? Well, income investors may be able to capitalize on the underlying uncertainty around SMCI's success, price volatility, and outlook. SMCI is in a highly competitive space and is constantly lumped against notable peers such as NVIDIA (NVDA).

Super Micro Computer's price has been in a near-constant decline over the last twelve months. This can be attributed to the fact that SMCY has experienced a similar price decline. The key to making SMCY a successful investment would be to time your entry in anticipation of when SMCI's price stabilizes. If SMCI's price decline eventually stops and stabilizes, SMCY will be a much more attractive investment. The high level of income may even allow investors to outperform common shareholders of SMCI if the price continues to have choppy trading sessions over the short term. So let's first start by taking a look at the fund's strategy and approach to generating income.

Fund Strategy & Risks

SMCY operates a synthetic option strategy to generate the necessary income that it needs to fund the high distribution rate. The term 'synthetic' refers to the fact that SMCY doesn't actually hold any of the underlying common shares of Super Micro Computer. This can elevate the risk profile and cause SMCY to trade with a greater amount of volatility. SMCY essentially mimics the daily price changes of SMCI by simultaneously buying SMCI call options and selling SMCI put options. The most recent prospectus lays out the different option strategies implemented pretty clearly.

I like the fact that SMCY makes use of different OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategies because it allows for a greater amount of price appreciation to be captured. For simplicity's sake, let's say that SMCI's price currently trades at $40 per share. SMCY may write an option against SMCI and select a strike price of $45 per share. If SMCI has a strong earnings report and the price spikes up to $50 per share, SMCY would capture the growth up to the strike price of $45 per share. Even though the gains above the strike price would essentially be sacrificed, SMCY would still capture a meaningful amount of upside.

The downside that we've been experiencing is that SMCY partakes in the full weight of downside movements. So unlike the capped upside, the downward risks are much more present. If SMCI's price continues to decline back to prior lows, SMCY could continue to reach new lows. Since SMCY's inception, the share price of Super Micro Computer has fallen by about 7%. Over the same period, SMCY's price has declined by nearly 45% since it has struggled to recover from the initial drop in late October, caused by several headwinds. SMCI's price was on the decline due to issues such as a delayed 10-Q filing, mixed reactions to an announced stock split, and its prior Q1 earnings.

This reveals the first downside to YieldMax funds: price decay. The limited upside potential caused by the option strategies makes it very difficult to see a price recovery following a rapid price decline. SMCY isn't the only fund to experience such an issue, but it has the unique disadvantage of these issues happening right after SMCY's initial launch. Just as a reference, we can see that there are plenty of other YieldMax funds that seem to be stuck in the downward price trend. Obviously, each fund aims to track a different underlying company, but I want this to demonstrate the issue and potential risk that a position in SMCY would entail.

While there are some YieldMax funds that have appreciated in value over time, those funds are definitely part of the minority. For reference, some of the funds that have increased in share price are the following:

  • YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY)
  • YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY)
  • YieldMax META Option Income Strategy ETF (FBY)

The high income received from SMCY would definitely come at a price if the price doesn't eventually stabilize, and there is a lot of uncertainty surrounding SMCI's future that investors must take into consideration. I wouldn't recommend SMCY for investors that have a high value of capital preservation. Lastly, SMCY's assets under management only amount to a little over $112M. There is a risk of liquidation if the price continues to decline and investors pull their capital out of the fund.

Super Micro Computer Uncertainty

SMCI's price has now declined by nearly 45% over the last twelve months. The stock faced several internal and external headwinds that caused the price to continuously slide. However, I believe that the underlying fundamentals are strong, which makes SMCI a solid long-term play. In the short term, SMCY shareholders can benefit from the elevated volatility. Some of the issues that have contributed to the price decline consist of the following:

  1. SMCI was investigated by the DOJ.
  2. No evidence of fraud was found.
  3. Q1 earnings were lower than Wall St. hoped for and shares declined.
  4. Delayed filing of its Form 10-K.
  5. SMCI discloses Nasdaq non-compliance letter.
  6. Removed from the Nasdaq-100 Index and replaced by Palantir (PLTR).
  7. Negatively impacted price movement from DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK).

While I am not debating whether or not the validity of these issues is a good reason to sell, I do understand why it has investors spooked. It makes sense why investors would be hesitant to invest their hard-earned money into a company that has their auditor, EY, resign from them. EY stated that it was unwilling to be a part of the financial reporting that SMCI provided.

However, I still see these things as short-term noise that doesn't change the underlying financial strength that the company has demonstrated. The company earned an A- grade, driven by its massive revenue, EPS, and EBITDA year-over-year growth. The latest earnings update indicates that EPS came in between $0.58 and $0.60 per share and revenue increased 54% year over year, falling within a range of $5.6B to $5.7B. The business ended the quarter with $1.4B in cash on hand, putting SMCI in a great position to maneuver headwinds and invest in new opportunities for growth.

With these results in mind, the company still trades at an extremely attractive valuation. SMCI also earns an A- grade on valuation due to its current forward price-to-earnings ratio of 16.69x, which significantly undercuts the sector median price-to-earnings ratio of 29.72x. Additionally, SMCI trades at a price-to-book ratio of 3.52x, which also undercuts the sector median of 4.46x.

Forward-looking, SMCI ramped up their production of NVIDIA's Blackwell, which can drive additional growth once these efforts materialize. These are systems that are efficient scaling of AI workloads, which is aided by improved cooling capacities. SMCI continues to align itself to benefit from the rising demand for different AI innovations. The size of the AI market is estimated to grow at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 27.67% through 2030. SMCI can continue to leverage its partnership with NVIDIA to capture a greater amount of market share year over year.

Dividend Utilization

SMCI doesn't offer investors a dividend at the moment, so SMCY makes it possible to collect income where it usually isn't. As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $1.7215 per share, the current dividend yield sits as high as 97% at the moment. However, we can already see that the distribution amounts have been on the decline, following the same trajectory as the underlying price movement. Although the payouts have been inconsistent recently, we can see increases over time, assuming that SMCI's price stabilizes and starts to move in some positive direction.

Additionally, the amount of the distribution is determined by factors such as the underlying stock's performance, time until expiration, efficiency of the options, and implied volatility levels. The higher the uncertainty, the higher the premiums are. Therefore, there's a chance that we gradually see an uptick in the distribution payouts in the short term. Management confirmed that they would be able to get all reporting finalized by the Feb 25th deadline.

This distribution history is so short, but assuming that it remains within the same range, I believe that the high amount of dividend income collected can be put to good use. I, personally, implement a dividend wheel strategy with all of the dividend income I collect from different YieldMax funds. I collect these dividends and then allocate them to other areas of my portfolio that may offer a better value proposition for a long-term investment.

For instance, I may allocate the dividends towards consumer-facing companies that are impacted by uncertainty around Trump's tariffs or different real estate investments that remain at suppressed valuations because of higher interest rates. This strategy helps me build up my core long-term positions at a much faster rate. In this specific instance, I may even use SMCY distributions to help me accumulate more shares of SMCI as the long-term potential still looks attractive. An alternative approach would be to accumulate all of the distributions received until you reach 'house money' status. This is when all distributions received total more than your initial investment amount. Since SMCY's projected annual yield is around 97%, investors should technically be able to collect over 100% of their initial investment amount back in a little over a year's time.

However, the distributions received will have some tax consequences that investors must take into consideration. The latest Section 19A notice is dated from October, which may no longer be an accurate representation. Using it as an example, we can see that SMCY's distributions were funded by 41% net investment income and nearly 59% of return of capital. Distributions funded by net investment income are typically classified as ordinary dividends, which have the most unfavorable tax consequences.

Return of capital distributions don't typically have immediate tax consequences if they aren't considered income. ROC distributions are used when the fund hasn't earned enough income to actually support the distribution. However, ROC distributions reduce your cost basis, meaning that you are basically deferring your taxes until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, SMCY could be an effective way to capture a high level of income from the underlying uncertainty revolving around Super Micro Computer. I have initiated positions in both SMCY and SMCI so that I can benefit from the short-term volatility and long-term potential. Although the price has rapidly deteriorated since inception, SMCY had unfortunate timing with its launch, as SMCI went through a series of headwinds that drove the price lower. Despite these challenges, SMCI has delivered strong financial results, with revenue growth of 54% year over year. Once the market recognizes this strong growth, momentum can shift to a more positive outlook. A more positive outlook would stop SMCY's price bleed and create a more favorable environment that allows better price stability and income generation.

data.title: Why Growth In U.S. Crude Exports Is Synonymous With Growth Of NYMEX WTI
data.description:
By Amanda Townsley

At a Glance

  • Over the past 15 years, Cushing has seen a dramatic increase in its tankage and pipeline connectivity, solidifying its position as the world's largest crude oil hub
  • Crude oil traded across North America is negotiated and transacted as a differential to the WTI futures contract

2025 marks the 10th anniversary of the lifting of the U.S. crude oil export ban, a development that helped launch North America into the top echelon of global oil suppliers while also recasting the landscape for crude oil trading.

Previously, a system designed for importing crude oil, the North American grid flipped outward, upending price dynamics and forming new trading hubs. As the North American crude oil trading system has adapted to its place in the global pricing system, here's a look at what's changed, what hasn't and what might be next.

The push to export crude oil after a 40-year ban resulted from burgeoning production and a race to build the infrastructure to efficiently deliver it to market. From a low of 8 million barrels per day in 2008, Canadian and U.S. oil supply grew to 13 million in 2015, and rose to 18 million in 2024. Over that period, production that had been dwindling began pushing the onshore pipeline system to capacity, requiring major investment to reroute flows and deliver the growing supply to refineries, trading hubs and coastal docks for export.

At the heart of the vast North American crude oil trading system is the NYMEX Light Sweet Crude Oil futures contract, often called "WTI". The price of WTI futures represents the outright price of a barrel of domestic light sweet crude oil at Cushing, Oklahoma. Cushing has been an effective delivery location for crude oil since the 1980s, as a vibrant hub for cash market trading of crude oil with an unparalleled network of pipelines, refineries and storage terminals.

If the Cushing of the 1980s was a "vibrant" hub, the Cushing of the last decade would be akin to a thriving metropolis. As the epicenter of both the pipeline and trading system, Cushing's tankage and pipeline connectivity has ballooned along with the North American grid over the last 15 years. The pipeline system channels crude oil from Western Canada, the Rockies, Bakken, Permian and regional plays into Cushing, where it can be stored, blended or delivered to local refineries and onto the Gulf Coast. In 2024, Cushing was home to 98 million barrels of crude oil storage capacity, making it the largest single hub in the world. Over 20 inbound pipelines, 16 outbound pipelines and more than 30 intra-Cushing lines provide unrivaled connectivity between a diverse mix of operators, across terminals and between production basins and other market hubs like the Gulf Coast.

Despite recent low inventory levels, records are being set at Cushing. In 2024, WTI futures set a record for physical deliveries of over 317K contracts, or 317 million barrels, the highest level since 2017. WTI futures rank number two globally for physical deliveries, behind the GME's Oman futures contract, and in 2024 saw twice the volume exchanged for barrels of the physically delivered Gulf Coast futures contract.

Whether global oil inventories remain near the low end of the historical range or build back toward historical highs, the ample export capacity and responsive pricing system mean that strong correlations between U.S., Cushing, and global inventories will continue.

WTI: So Much More than Crude at Cushing

As Cushing is the heart of the oil pipeline system, the WTI futures contract is the heart of the North American trading system.

Centered around the price of crude oil at Cushing is a long-established trading ecosystem that allows traders to efficiently price oil no matter its location or quality. Crude oil traded anywhere from Hardisty, Alberta, to Corpus Christi, Texas, is negotiated and transacted as a differential, or spread, to the WTI futures contract. The spread at which a specific grade of crude oil trades versus the NYMEX price reflects differences in quality, transportation costs and regional supply-demand variables. Traders carefully measure these factors to properly value different types of crude oil, as well as to route their crude oil to the most profitable destination.

Spot physical crude oil trades actively during the month before it will be delivered. Price reporting agencies such as Argus Media and Platts aggregate transactions for various grades on a daily basis and publish a daily differential index price. Price differentials are typically within a few dollars above or below NYMEX WTI, but certain grades or locations can trade at double-digit discounts. The price a seller will receive for the crude oil will be NYMEX WTI plus the differential at which the trade is executed. Rather than transacting at a fixed differential, producers and consumers can also choose to buy or sell crude at the index price: the monthly average of the differential published by the price reporting agency plus the monthly average of NYMEX WTI.

The Rise of Grades: Hedging a Differential to WTI

The trading ecosystem aggregates the majority of price discovery and liquidity at a single location: WTI futures. This allows the bulk of risk management and hedging to occur nearly around the clock, with low cost and high transparency. This also means that most of the time, fluctuations of the price of crude at Cushing via WTI futures is the main driver of fluctuations in the price of crude oil produced, traded or exported across North America.

Even before the shale boom, the large geographic footprint and quality diversity in North American crude oil gave rise not just to active physical trading in other hubs, but also demand for crude oil hedging instruments beyond domestic light sweet at Cushing.

In 2007, NYMEX launched a series of futures contracts to meet this need, offering traders the ability to hedge the price differentials around the U.S. The initial futures contracts cash-settled to the prices of Mars, West Texas Sour (WTS), and Louisiana Light Sweet (LLS) published by Argus Media, the same differentials that underpinned most physical transactions.

The expansion of the U.S. pipeline network and crude supply in the following decade led to the birth of new grades of crude oil and new trading hubs in North America. As both a refining center and a geographically convenient outlet for exports, the focus on Houston along the Gulf Coast grew. With strong quality parameters and broad connectivity to Permian production and the refining network, Magellan's East Houston terminal (also called "MEH") emerged as the hub. In 2015, Argus began assessing "WTI Houston" - the price differential to NYMEX WTI of WTI Midland-quality crude oil at MEH. The WTI Houston assessment quickly became the standard of value for light sweet crude oil on the Gulf Coast. Any trader that buys or sells crude oil on the Gulf Coast today will look to the Argus WTI Houston differential plus NYMEX WTI as the key indicator for the value of that crude oil. The outright price of crude oil on the Gulf Coast cannot be determined without the price of NYMEX WTI.

A CME Group futures contract for the Argus WTI Houston differential to WTI, known by the industry as its exchange code "HTT", began trading in 2016. With the Argus WTI Houston differential and the NYMEX WTI futures contracts as the underlying prices for physical transactions, HTT was welcomed by the industry as a convenient hedge for the quality and location of the growing grade of crude.

The flexibility to add or subtract new grades and locations as the market shifts is a hallmark and benefit of the U.S. crude trading system. In 2025, open interest in these types of grades futures contracts, priced as differentials to NYMEX WTI, is approaching 800 million barrels and extends out four years. Beyond WTI and Brent, this represents more barrels than any other oil futures contract globally and highlights both CME's HTT futures contract as the instrument of choice for managing risk on the U.S. Gulf Coast, and NYMEX WTI as the central pricing mechanism for crude oil.

What WTI Means for the Rest of the World

The sheer scale of U.S. oil export capability has meant that U.S. crude oil prices have influenced crude oil prices globally for years. The WTI-Brent futures spread reflects the price difference between the U.S. and North Sea crude oil markets. At the start of the shale boom, the spread flipped from a positive value to as low as negative $25 per barrel. By 2020, the build out of U.S. export infrastructure narrowed the spread, with WTI trading $3 to $5 under Brent during 2024.

As a nod to the significance of U.S. crude supply and the decline in European production, in 2023 U.S. crude oil was added to the basket of deliverable grades under the Brent futures contract. According to Argus and Platts, U.S. WTI Midland crude oil - priced off of WTI futures - has since been setting the price of Dated Brent more than 50% of the time. This shift made explicit that anyone with exposure to the Brent complex also has exposure to U.S. NYMEX WTI crude prices, and led to the adoption of NYMEX WTI-based cargo pricing for European refiners.

In the tenth year of North America's development as a global crude oil export powerhouse, some changes are only just beginning. Given the meteoric rise in North American exports, international use of WTI continues to grow. Trading volume of WTI originating outside the U.S. hit a high of 37% during 2024, up from 29% year-on-year, and the majority of new participants in both WTI futures and CME's WTI Houston futures came from outside of the U.S.

The NYMEX WTI Light Sweet crude oil futures contract has been around for 42 years, serving as the benchmark for U.S. oil prices and underpinning the prices of consumer goods like gasoline, diesel and propane - and the price of all types of crude oil around North America and even overseas. With crude oil on the Gulf Coast valued and traded as a differential to WTI futures, growth in the importance of Gulf Coast crude oil is synonymous with the WTI Houston marker and the WTI futures contract that sits at its core. NYMEX WTI has truly gone global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet Q4: Mixed Results And A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) shares after the Q4 earnings release. This article is an update to my introductory article published on December 12 with the same recommendation.

Alphabet is a pioneer and leader in the search and advertising market. We will see below that the company's results were mixed in 4Q24, with concerns about the cloud business and investments, partially offset by the excellent performance of YouTube. However, the valuation seems fair.

Google Q4 Results Review

Alphabet released its earnings on February 4. While there were some positive highlights, consolidated revenues came in below expectations.

After the earnings release, the company's shares fell 8%. From now on, we will analyze the results and talk about topics that I see few analysts addressing. Enjoy your reading!

Revenues - Below Expectations

Alphabet reported net revenue of $96.47 billion (+12% y/y), slightly below the $96.67 billion expected by the market. We will see below that the search advertising business and YouTube brought strong results. However, there are concerns about the cloud business and other bets.

Search ad revenue grew 13% YoY to $54 billion, driven by AI-generated summaries that boosted engagement. YouTube’s ad revenue grew 14% to $10.5 billion, also driven by AI-enhanced content recommendations and political ads. Total ad revenue surpassed $72 billion, which in my view reaffirms Google’s dominance in the industry.

However, it was also notable that Google Cloud’s net revenue increased 30% YoY to $12 billion, falling short of the estimate of $12.16 billion. Google Cloud still lags behind AWS and Microsoft in market share.

Finally, the “other” segment, which includes the Verily science unit and the Waymo self-driving car unit, reported revenue of $400 million, down from $657 million in the previous quarter.

Costs and Expenses - Good Control

The company's annual costs rose 8.09%, below the 11.77% growth in revenues, and as a result, there was a gain in gross margin. Meanwhile, sales and operating expenses were also contained, which contributed to a 30% growth in operating income.

Although cost and expense control is satisfactory, investments (CAPEX) have caught the attention of investors and can be considered one of the risks to the thesis. We will see more below.

CapEx - Above Expectations By Investors

Investments were $14.3 billion, above the expected $13.2 billion. But that wasn't what caught my attention, even after the DeepSeek event, Alphabet announced significant investments of $75 billion.

The market estimated that CapEx would be $57.9 billion, which raises questions about investments regarding the high expenditures on AI and its ability to monetize it. Executives justified that the company should allocate a large part of the investments to data centers, which will support the growth of the company's business.

Net Income - Positive Surprise

On the bottom line, the company's net income grew 28% YoY to $26.54 billion in 4Q24. While earnings per share (EPS) rose 31% to $2.15, slightly above expectations of $2.13 as we can see below.

My assessment is that the results released were mixed, with results below expectations in the cloud segment, skepticism about CapEx, partially offset by a good performance from YouTube and a good control of costs and expenses in my view. Let's look at the valuation.

Valuation - Cheap

Despite considering the mixed result, the same does not occur with the valuation, which I consider very cheap for the quality of the company. Let's see the historical P/E multiple below.

We can see above that the company traded at its highest at 35x earnings and at its lowest at 17x earnings, for an average of 26x earnings. Currently, the company trades at 20.8x earnings, an upside of 25% that corroborates my buy recommendation.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

The company faces challenges from cost-cutting, regulatory challenges and an internal crisis of confidence. At the end of 3Q24, for example, CFO Anat Ashkenazi said she intended to “push a little further” in reducing expenses, while balancing rising spending on AI infrastructure.

In this sense, employees began to question executives for details about the layoffs planned for 2025. In January, a group of Alphabet employees even created an internal petition called "job security". It was then that the company began offering voluntary redundancy packages to employees in the Platforms and Devices unit, which has more than 25,000 employees.

As for artificial intelligence, after the launch of ChatGPT, Alphabet rebranded its portfolio to "Gemini". In February 2024, for example, Google launched Imagen 2, an AI image generation tool. Users immediately spotted historical errors. The product was taken offline and relaunched just six months later as Imagen 3.

Another blow was AI Overview, a feature that synthesized answers directly from Google search, which also had glaring errors. The failure raised concerns in the market about the accuracy of Google's AI products.

Finally, in addition to the company being under trial by the US Department of Justice (DOJ) to divest its Chrome and Android browsers, the company has also recently become the target of an investigation by China for antitrust violations. The risks are numerous, and investors should exercise caution.

The Bottom Line

Alphabet reported mixed results in 4Q24. While businesses like YouTube posted good numbers, cloud businesses came in below expectations and contributed to revenue falling short of estimates.

In addition, there is skepticism among investors regarding the company's strong $75 billion in CapEx investments for 2025, mainly focused on AI. This skepticism is justified, given the problems the company has already faced with Imagen2 initiatives and AI Overview.

However, when we analyze its valuation, we see a robust 25% upside potential for a company that is known for its quality, creativity and ability to deliver great results.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my buy recommendation on Alphabet shares. I see a company consistently surprising the market in earnings estimates, its cost and expense control is very positive amid strong investments in AI. The risk/return ratio seems very positive.

data.title: Cheniere Energy: The Only Pure LNG Player Worth It
data.description:
Overview

Cheniere Energy (NYSE:LNG) is one of the largest players in the LNG industry. It currently operates two critical LNG facilities: its legacy Sabine Pass LNG Terminal in Louisiana and the newer Corpus Christi LNG Terminal. Cheniere currently has a market cap of around $50 billion and has a combined production capacity of approx. 45 million tonnes per annum that could further reach 55 MTPA with the newest expansion at Corpus Christi.

Cheniere was one of the earliest believers in LNG in the United States. It was originally founded in 1996 to import gas but the rise of shale gas changed market dynamics resulting in an abundant domestic gas market. In 2010, Cheniere then took the bold step of pivoting Sabine Pass to an exporting terminal, taking the first step into making the U.S. the LNG champion that it is now. Keep in mind that Qatar, the previous number 1, has been exporting LNG since 1996 yet was taken over by America in less than a decade thanks largely to the boldness of Cheniere Energy.

Industry Outlook

The demand for LNG has grown tremendously over the years with recent catalysts such as the Russian invasion of Ukraine, which made Europe reliant on steady LNG imports. The region became the biggest customer for Cheniere replacing legacy Asian countries. Now, it is important to note that his trend has slightly reversed as Asia is regaining demand share as countries such as Japan and India increase their LNG imports.

Cheniere can also benefit tremendously from the new administration, as Trump lifted restrictions on the sector and has been vocal about the importance of the O&G industry. It is also important to note that, contrary to popular belief, Cheniere benefited from the Biden administration, as it stopped/delayed entries from competitors such as Energy Transfer (ET). This means that Cheniere faces less competition in the near term. S&P expects LNG export capacity to double over the next five years and Cheniere's leadership gives it a first-mover advantage, albeit not as strong as earlier in the LNG cycle.

Another point of contention would be gas prices, S&P believes that Henry Hub prices will average more than $4.00 MMBtu in 2025. Cheniere needs to have gas prices stay low for better margins, as it will benefit from lower feed gas costs while selling at long-term contracted rates.

Valuation

Cheniere boasts a respectable price-to-earnings GAAP ratio of 13.96, 8.17% higher than the sector median. Otherwise, the company is quite expensive on other metrics. P/B is extremely high at 9.68x, accompanied by a high debt at ~$27 billion. While this would tend to keep me away, the company keeps an asset-light business model as it is unlike any other traditional O&G companies. Cheniere does not have larger reserves of natural gas, and is solely an infrastructure and trading company.

What is impressive? Growth. Since the first LNG shipment in 2016, Cheniere has been growing sales at a massive 36.57% CAGR. Revenue peaked in 2022 due to the massive rally for natural gas; however, prices have now stabilized. Cheniere was also able to capitalize on short-term agreements that have now been converted to long-term contracts with lower per-unit revenues. During that time, net income went from -$610 million to $3,652 billion, which in turn sustains the dividend growth the company has been offering, passing from $0.66 in 2021 to $1.81 per share.

The company also revised its 2024 guidance with consolidated EBITDA reaching between $6 and $6.3 billion, while distributable cash flow reaching $3.4-3.7 billion. The guidance was revised thanks to higher production and margins. For 2025, Cheniere expects Stage 3 of the expansion of the Corpus Christi Terminal to reach substantial completion. Furthermore, the LNG company forecasts 47–48 million tonnes of LNG produced across both terminals.

Looking at Capital Return other than the dividend, Cheniere bought back close to $2 billion of the company's outstanding shares in the first three quarters of 2024. In June, the Board authorized an additional $4 billion for share repurchases through 2027. As of today, the company has reduced 8.29% of its share count in two years. Cheniere also focused on strengthening its balance sheet by repaying $4 billion of long-term debt.

A peer comparison might be tricky for Cheniere, as it is the only public U.S. company with a pure LNG focus. Tellurian was bought out by Australia's Woodside Energy in July 2024, and Venture Global (VG) only IPO'd three weeks ago. In addition, VG settled for a price tag close to 45% lower than the $110 billion it aimed for, and has since lost a further $22 billion in valuation.

Taking all the points above into my valuation assessment, Cheniere is slightly expensive, but I'll say that it is expensive for a reason - the growth has been unbelievable, and it is the only company that has long-established terminals that are generating tremendous amounts of FCF and reduced CapEx.

Risks

Cheniere is exposed to natural gas LNG prices. If Henry Hub prices rise way above $4/MMBtu and LNG prices remain flat, Cheniere could see its margins squeezed. Plus, an oversupply could also drive prices down, especially with Qatar's expansion. However, Cheniere mitigates this with over 90% of its volumes sold at fixed prices.

Competition is also growing, with more and more projects coming online before 2030. The demand needs to increase for Cheniere to keep its advantage. Qatar is also aiming to produce 126 MTPA by 2027, potentially flooding the market if demand does not tick up.

LNG being positioned as a transition fuel could also harm its distant future prospects, as long-term shifts toward renewables could reduce demand growth beyond the 2040s. However, some of the biggest LNG users such as China and India still see LNG as a cleaner alternative to coal, supporting its long-term viability.

Conclusion

I would rate Cheniere Energy as a mix between a Hold and Buy. On the one side, Cheniere stock has appreciated tremendously in the last couple of years, and I don't know if it will offer the same kind of returns in the near future. Yet, I like how it is positioned in the worldwide LNG market with a bespoke offering of top-of-the-line cash-generating assets. It is the most reliable option to gain exposure to one of the most interesting market dynamics in the O&G industry. A slight pullback in the share price will be a good entry point for this stock.

data.title: Janus Henderson Global Real Estate Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

Global equities markets were flat in the fourth quarter. November was the strongest month, as Donald Trump’s convincing win in the U.S. presidential election sparked a U.S. rally, with investors hoping that his economic policies would spur growth and lower taxes.

Gains reversed in December as hawkish comments from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) and the resultant sharp rise in U.S. Treasury yields weighed heavily on investor sentiment. The Fed said it expected to make fewer interest rate cuts in 2025 because inflation would likely take longer to return to below its 2% target. In Europe, U.S. President-elect Trump’s threat to implement trade tariffs against the European Union and political upheaval in European economic powerhouses Germany and France sapped investor morale, leading to weak equity market returns.

Overall, global listed real estate underperformed wider equity markets, declining 9.5% amid upheaval in bond markets. U.S. real estate investment trusts (REITs) had a challenging quarter, falling 6.6% after a more hawkish tone from the Fed about further cuts sent shock waves through the bond market in December, with 10-year U.S. Treasury bond (US10Y) yields hitting a six-month high of 4.57%. German, UK and Swedish property stocks were particularly weak, posting double-digit declines driven by political instability, rising bond yields, and worries over possible tariffs. Asian property stocks also saw sharp declines, although Japan held in on a relative basis despite material yen weakness after The Bank of Japan (BOJ) decided not to raise interest rates at its December policy meeting, adopting a less hawkish stance. In the U.S., at a property sector level most sectors posted negative returns, with rate-sensitive sectors such as cell towers, self-storage, and industrials hit hardest. Cyclical areas of the property market outperformed, notably malls and hotels. Residential sectors were also relative outperformers, as higher base rates tend to translate into higher mortgage rates – a positive for demand in all forms of rental housing.

Portfolio review

Hotel operator Wyndham Hotels & Resorts (WH) was a key contributor to outperformance, following another set of strong results and signs of inflecting demand for economy and mid-scale hotels. Data center landlord Equinix (EQIX) and retail mall REIT Macerich (MAC) also contributed positively, benefiting from a pro-growth rally in U.S. equities following Trump’s decisive election victory.

Conversely, cold-storage owner/operator Lineage (LINE) detracted from relative returns, with debut results showing some softness in fundamentals following its summer initial public offering. Public Storage (PSA) also hurt relative returns in what was a difficult quarter for the storage sector, with sentiment impacted by a less favorable interest rate environment that drove mortgage rates higher. Multi-family residential landlord Canadian Apartment Properties (OTC:CDPYF) also detracted following the Canadian government’s announcement of immigration cuts to curb population growth.

Manager outlook

In most markets globally, we believe we are now at an inflection point for real estate, following a two-year correction in the wake of rising interest rates. Confidence that interest rates have peaked and with many central banks now easing, we anticipate a much more supportive backdrop for real estate.

We expect public REITs to continue to lead the recovery, boosted by more exposure to winning real estate sectors, lower leverage, and a cost- and access-to-capital advantage providing a pathway for growth. Importantly, public REITs have continued to offer reliable and growing income streams, a characteristic that we think could continue to reward investors.

Portfolio management

Guy Barnard, CFA | Tim Gibson | Greg Kuhl, CFA

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Where Gold Has Gone, Will Silver Follow? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Gold has been one of my favorite plays for some time. I’ve called the yellow metal a great “Be Bold” investment at multiple MoneyShows and in columns in your newsletters. And it has worked out great. The SPDR Gold Shares (NYSEARCA:GLD) has surged 10% year-to-date... and 43% in the last year!

But now, I’m asking myself a simple question: Where gold has gone, will silver follow?

Look at the first MoneyShow Chart of the Day. You can see GLD is hitting new high after new high. It’s doing so on solid volume, and the Relative Strength Index (RSI) is confirming the move. The 50-day and 200-day moving averages are also on the up and up.

SPDR Gold Shares (GLD)

(Data by YCharts)

But what about the iShares Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:SLV)? It’s not bad - up a little more (11%) YTD, but a little less (41%) over the past 12 months. Momentum isn’t as strong.

iShares Silver Trust (SLV)

(Data by YCharts)

Most importantly, we have not hit a new high. That’s true over the past few months... and over the long term. SLV hit its modern-day high back in 2011.

In a world of rising uncertainty, volatility, and central bank metals buying... plus solid demand from the industrial, solar, and electronics industries... and greater investor demand for wealth protection and currency debasement insurance... can silver really keep lagging?

Or in simple terms, where gold has gone, will silver follow?
